title: The Old Forest and Other Stories
author: Max Berengaut
body: The first Peter Taylor story I read was from In The Miro District and it was about a rich family in Tennessee and a sister (or daughter) who marries a young man, the brother (or son) explains them and finds through explanation the reason the sister and her husband disappear to their room every day and don't work is because they're alcoholics who drink all day -- what I liked was how the narrator lazily says somewhere near the end that he'd started his first real drinking then. I must have been in college come to think of it, I liked how the story was about alcoholism but not really brazen about it. I'm older now, Taylor feels more specific than he did then (though I only read one story). Most of these stories are about Southern feelings of identity and family dynamics, some cultural historicity here and there but really that's what he mostly writes about. He has a really calm, methodical, and precise writing style. I really like the long sentences that don't have commas but are unique and aesthetically pleasing. Probably one of the better mid-20th century American writers that I've read. My three favorites stories (The Old Forest, A Long Fourth, The Scoutmaster) are really beautiful works of art. The Old Forest has this like 15 page explanation of a specific type of Southern woman and their cultural demise at the turn of the 60s that's really amazing, like shockingly good. He's also pretty unabashed in his portrayal of race in these rich Southern families who all have black servants, in the sense he has no bones describing them as part of the family but in a subjugated way he doesn't try to explain away or make more politically correct; it's a product of its times as he wrote in the mid-20th century, that he doesn't try to compensate for it elsewhere, but it's also something of an artistic choice because he is really fundamentally interested in depicting these lives as they actually were and believes that in their full and earnest description he is doing something worthwhile. For example, A Long Fourth contains a messy relationship between a rich mother who frequently espouses out loud her low opinions of black people, then has a rough interaction with her longtime servant because the servant compares her own troubled nephew to the mother's son (who is returning for the fourth of July from up north where it's implied he is getting northernized) and the mother can't take a black person comparing another black person to her son, she sees it as an affront as she hates the nephew and it psychologically troubles her. Her daughters who have remained at home and are in their late 20s read fancy intellectual magazines, but when her son arrives with a young woman who people don't understand if they're together are not, the daughters say really horrible racist shit which surprises the mother and the son and his perhaps girlfriend respond, with the son saying things that express clear disdain for his family and later it's seen his perhaps girlfriend, things that may be correct about how southerners are backwards but are also quite cold as it's his own family, and the mother suddenly realizes that her servant was right to compare the nephew to her son as both are cold to their own "kin" in heartless ways. All of this coalesces really well formally speaking. It's the openness to displaying the hardness to black people in the southerners' minds, and the silver linings to the hardness in the sense that they genuinely care about them (not in the Gosford Park let's gossip about the servants now way) because the black servants are part of their senses of self, that he is interested in depicting because he feels it's the truth of what his mind has most clearly observed more than anyone else. But it's still kind of annoying, the race stuff, so of my three favorite, only one of them has stuff related to it and the other stories in the collection do have it -- i would say like 60 or 65%. The Old Fourth I've already explained the importance of but even there the saddest thing about it is that no one cares about this type of young woman anymore, and he doesn't (and I don't) think it's sad in the sense that cultural values are deteriorating and women who are willing to make real sacrifices for their men at their own expense are no longer valued by society because society has grown rotten, no it's that it's sad because this type of woman is adrift (at that time, I feel it's not this way now, where maybe women are able to feel power in their filial duty to men) and quasi-powerless and it's just the way that the world works. That's about searching for truth and finding artistic inspiration, or perhaps it's the other way around, I don't know. The third story The Scoutmaster made me realize all of his stories sort of redound to this idea of a fading sense of Southern pride and cultural value amongst the rich, and the importance of this pride, but on its own before it got to that final coda it was really interesting in its frank description of the familial relationships of a certain family and it felt so full, it felt like witnessing something with the weight of life, rich and vast, sort of like another in this collection 'The Little Cousins' which wasn't as good on a formal level but also described debutante society very well. He probably liked F. Scott Fitzgerald, I'm thinking now.
